name: Raymond Kowalski
chief_complaint: My leg is all red and swollen and it's spreading fast. I feel like crap, honestly.
onset_description: Noticed a small red area on his left shin 3 days ago after scratching a bug bite. Over the past 24 hours the redness has spread rapidly up the leg, and he developed fevers and chills last night.

leg_redness_and_swelling.description: My whole lower leg is red and hot to the touch, it's way bigger than it was yesterday. I drew a line with a marker and it's past the line now.
leg_redness_and_swelling.onset: 3 days ago, rapidly worsening over 24 hours
leg_redness_and_swelling.character: spreading erythema with well-demarcated borders, warm, edematous, tender
leg_redness_and_swelling.location: left lower leg, from ankle to just below knee

fever.description: I've been running a fever since last night, felt like I was burning up
alleviating_factors: took Tylenol at home with minimal effect

chills.description: Shivering spells that come and go, especially when the fever spikes

malaise.description: I feel wiped out, just exhausted. No energy at all.
malaise.character: profound fatigue and general ill feeling

leg_pain.description: The whole shin is throbbing. Feels like it's going to burst.
leg_pain.character: throbbing, constant, with tenderness to touch

labs.CBC: WBC 16.8 (elevated with left shift, 8% bands), Hgb 14.2, Plt 212
labs.wound_culture: Pending (obtained from area of bullae drainage)

past_medical_history: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (12 years, poorly controlled)
Peripheral venous insufficiency, bilateral lower extremities
Previous cellulitis left leg (2 years ago)

social_history.smoking: Current smoker, 1 pack per day for 30 years (30 pack-year history)
social_history.drugs: Denies current use, used marijuana in his 20s
social_history.occupation: Long-haul truck driver, sits for extended periods
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in an apartment, divorced 5 years ago, two adult children who live out of state
social_history.exercise: Very little, sedentary occupation and lifestyle
social_history.diet: Fast food most meals, admits to poor dietary habits. Rarely checks blood sugar.

conditions.father: Type 2 Diabetes, died at 64 from MI
conditions.brother: Type 2 Diabetes, had a below-knee amputation at age 54

personality: Gruff and somewhat impatient. Uses humor to deflect from how worried he actually is. Doesn't like being told what to do. Downplays his poor self-care but becomes defensive if pressed.
communication_style: Blunt and direct. Uses casual, sometimes crude language. Interrupts with questions. Reluctant to give detailed answers and prefers short responses. Gets annoyed with repetitive questions.
pain_description_style: Tends to use dramatic comparisons when the pain is bad ('feels like my leg is on fire') but will minimize it if he thinks it makes him look weak. Provides a number grudgingly if asked.

critical_findings: Rapidly spreading cellulitis with systemic signs (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis)
Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 9.4%) increasing infection risk
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP 124, procalcitonin 1.8) suggesting systemic infection
History of recurrent cellulitis suggesting chronic predisposing factors
Risk factors: obesity, venous insufficiency, tinea pedis, diabetes, smoking
Advancing erythema despite 3 days of progression
rubric.diagnosis: Cellulitis of left lower extremity with systemic inflammatory response
differential_diagnoses: Necrotizing fasciitis (must rule out given rapid spread and systemic toxicity)
Deep vein thrombosis with secondary cellulitis
Septic arthritis of ankle or knee
recommended_interventions: IV antibiotics (non-penicillin based due to allergy: vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or clindamycin)
Mark borders of erythema with time stamps to monitor progression
Wound culture if any drainage or bullae present
Surgical consult if concern for necrotizing fasciitis (pain out of proportion, crepitus, rapid spread)
Glycemic control with insulin sliding scale (hold oral agents)
DVT ultrasound of left lower extremity to rule out concurrent DVT
Diabetes education and outpatient follow-up planning
